coredata.dc:description: Minimally processed fruits and vegetables (MPFVs) experience significant quality degradation during storage due to oxygen exposure, mechanical damage, and microbial contamination, which significantly reduces shelf life and leads to substantial economic waste. This research developed a cost-effective and environmentally active packaging by incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from lemons into guar gum (GG) and sodium alginate (SA) films. The CQDs were integrated into the biopolymer matrix via simple film-casting techniques. The CQDs exhibited exceptional antioxidant and antibacterial properties due to the abundant functional groups and unique quantum effects. The integration of CQDs into GG/SA films enhanced UV-blocking capabilities, mechanical strength (38.80 MPa), and antioxidant activity (43.45%). The release kinetics of CQDs from the films followed the Fickian diffusion kinetics. The use of CQDs offers several advantages over conventional methods including their biocompatibility, sustainability, and multifunctionality. Additionally, the films effectively delayed the browning of blanched asparagus. The mechanism of browning inhibition was attributed to the prevention of chlorophyll degradation and enzymatic browning. This approach offers a sustainable and effective solution for extending the shelf life and safety of MPFVs.

head.ce:title: Lemon-derived carbon quantum dots incorporated guar gum/sodium alginate films with enhanced the preservability for blanched asparagus active packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Minimally processed fruits and vegetables (MPFVs) experience significant quality degradation during storage due to oxygen exposure, mechanical damage, and microbial contamination, which significantly reduces shelf life and leads to substantial economic waste. This research developed a cost-effective and environmentally active packaging by incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from lemons into guar gum (GG) and sodium alginate (SA) films. The CQDs were integrated into the biopolymer matrix via simple film-casting techniques. The CQDs exhibited exceptional antioxidant and antibacterial properties due to the abundant functional groups and unique quantum effects. The integration of CQDs into GG/SA films enhanced UV-blocking capabilities, mechanical strength (38.80 MPa), and antioxidant activity (43.45%). The release kinetics of CQDs from the films followed the Fickian diffusion kinetics. The use of CQDs offers several advantages over conventional methods including their biocompatibility, sustainability, and multifunctionality. Additionally, the films effectively delayed the browning of blanched asparagus. The mechanism of browning inhibition was attributed to the prevention of chlorophyll degradation and enzymatic browning. This approach offers a sustainable and effective solution for extending the shelf life and safety of MPFVs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Minimally processed fruits and vegetables (MPFVs) have gained substantial market traction due to their convenience and health benefits. Among them, blanched asparagus is popular for its ready-to-eat properties and high nutritional value. However, blanched asparagus presents unique preservation challenges owing to its particular susceptibility to enzymatic browning and microbial spoilage (Chang et al., 2023). Recent studies highlight that the primary mechanism driving browning in asparagus involves enzymatic browning, a reaction that catalyzes the oxidation of phenolic substrates into quinones, forming brown pigments that compromise both the aesthetic and nutritional integrity of the vegetable (Xiao et al., 2024). In addition, the processes of cutting and blanching compromise the structural integrity of asparagus. The exposed cut ends are particularly prone to retaining moisture, thereby creating optimal conditions for microbial reproduction. This not only accelerates spoilage but also raises significant food safety concerns (Cui, Cheng, Li, Khin, & Lin, 2023). Addressing these specific challenges is crucial for extending the shelf life of blanched asparagus, making it a focal point of research in active packaging solutions.

Active packaging can maintain the shelf life and quality of food by releasing or removing loaded nanomaterials (Wu et al., 2024). However, most of the nanomaterials are chemically synthesized that involve unpleasant odors, low toxicity, raising sustainability concerns and potential safety risks for food-contact applications (Bahrami, Delshadi, Assadpour, Jafari, & Williams, 2020).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are promising functional materials in various applications because of their excellent physicochemical characteristics, low toxicity, and biocompatibility (Marimuthu, Xu, Song, Chen, & Wen, 2025; Thakur et al., 2024). Notably, it was found that the carbon precursor significantly influences the behavior of CQDs because the carbonization process preserves the carbon source characteristics (P. Li et al., 2022). Lemons are rich in compounds such as citric acid and flavonoids, which can be incorporated or modified on the surface of CQDs with strong antibacterial and antioxidant capabilities during the synthesis process. In addition, peels of citrus fruits and the derived CQDs have been widely studied for their potential in cancer therapy (Basta, Lotfy, Ghaly, Nabil, & Mohamed, 2020), drug delivery (Basta & Lotfy, 2021), and as antioxidants in food packaging (Nazar, Hasan, Wirjosentono, & Gani, 2024). Their ability to interact with biological systems, combined with their non-toxic and biocompatible nature, makes them ideal candidates for a range of bioactive applications, particularly in food preservation and medicine (Ye et al., 2023). Last but not least, utilizing lemon waste as a carbon source not only enhances resource efficiency but also supports sustainable development goals (Ahuja et al., 2022). Compared to synthetic precursors or rare biomass sources, lemon-derived CQDs are cost-effective and environmentally sustainable, aligning with circular economy principles. Therefore, this study synthesized lemon-derived CQDs to explore their effects on browning in MPFVs after thermal processing, which is an area that has not been extensively investigated.

Meanwhile, growing concerns about the environmental and health impacts of synthetic plastics are driving interest in food packaging made from natural biopolymers with biodegradable, renewable, and non-toxic properties (Barone, Matheus, de Souza, Moreira, & Fai, 2021). Guar gum (GG) is a naturally derived polysaccharide that is extensively used in the food industry owing to its biodegradability and affordability (Yang, Li, Li, Huang, et al., 2024). However, single-component films often lack sufficient mechanical strength and barrier properties, which limits their practical applications. The development of composite films has been extensively studied (Tong et al., 2023). Sodium alginate (SA) is another polysaccharide that has been shown to strengthen the performance of packaging when combined with GG, making them more suitable for packaging (Rebouças et al., 2023). The role of GG and SA in food packaging has been well documented (Yang, Li, Li, Li, et al., 2024). They are frequently used in controlled release systems due to their biocompatibility and biodegradability, making them suitable for food preservation and even biomedical applications (Yang, Li, Li, Huang, et al., 2024). Therefore, we hypothesized that the combination of SA and GG would enhance the adhesion of the composite film to food surfaces, making it more suitable for the application and preservation of heat-processed vegetable-based dishes. Additionally, the incorporation of CQDs into the GG/SA films could help mediate the release of active ingredients, realize the delivery and slow release of active ingredients, and effectively reduce the browning.

In this study, CQDs were synthesized from lemons through a hydrothermal method and combined with GG and SA through in situ polymerization to create packaging films with superior preservation properties. The research focuses on evaluating the antimicrobial and antioxidant activities of the films with analyzing the release kinetics of the loaded CQDs. The effectiveness of these coated films was tested on blanched asparagus, examining the impact of CQDs on browning-related enzyme activity and chlorophyll retention. This innovative preservation mechanism offers a promising solution for the safe storage and extended shelf life of MPFVs.

Lemons were bought from Jimailong Supermarket in Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, China. Asparagus were obtained from RT-Mart Supermarket in Yixing, Jiangsu, China. Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were sourced from the Shanghai Conservation Microbiology Center, Shanghai, China. GG (M W = 2.5 × 105, purity > 82 %), SA (Mw = 1.5 × 105, G/M = 1:1), ABTS, DPPH, potassium persulfate, ethanol, catechol, guaiacol, forintol, acetone and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, Shanghai, China. Filtration membrane (0.22 μm) was acquired from Aladdin Biochemistry Technology Co. Ltd, Shanghai, China.

First, lemon peels were dried in an oven at 40 °C for 12 h and then powdered. 1.5 g of lemon peel powder was added to 30 mL of H2O and sonicated for 10 mins to ensure uniform dispersion. The suspension was moved to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 3 h, followed by cooling to room temperature (25 ± 3°C). The obtained solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 mins to eliminate non-fluorescent precipitates. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain the CQDs solution. Finally, the CQDs solution was freeze-dried to produce CQDs powder, which was reconstituted in ultrapure water to prepare the 1.0 mg/mL CQDs solution.

The microstructure of the CQDs was characterized by a transmission electron microscope (JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan). In order to obtain well-dispersed CQDs suitable for high-resolution imaging, an appropriate amount of CQDs were dispersed in deionized water and subjected to a brief ultrasonic treatment. The sample was then dropped onto a copper grid and the grid air-dried at room temperature.
At the same time, the appropriate amount of CQDs was dissolved in water and evenly dispersed. Zeta potential was assessed using a Malvern Zeta Potentiometer (Malvern, Worcestershire, UK).

CQDs was dissolved in deionized water and stirred to achieve a consistent concentration. UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded by a spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1601, Tokyo, Japan), while fluorescence spectra were obtained by using a spectrometer (Lengguang F97XP, Technology, Shanghai, China).

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed with a spectrometer (Nicolet 50 spectrometer, Boston, USA) utilizing an ATR accessory. The CQDs powder was placed directly in the ATR accessory to record the spectrum in the range of 4000–525 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenging activities of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The CQD solution was diluted to the desired concentration. Subsequently, the CQD solution (1 mL) was thoroughly mixed with 2.5 mL of DPPH ethanol solution or ABTS solution. After incubating in the dark for 20 mins, the absorbance of DPPH was measured at 517 nm and the absorbance of ABTS was measured at 734 nm. The radical scavenging rates of DPPH and ABTS were computed according to the following formula:
(1)
Radical Scavenging Rates
( % )
=
A 0
- A
i
A 0
× 100
where Ai and A0 represent the absorbance of the sample and the blank.

The active packaging films were prepared with the casting method. Initially, 0.5 g of GG and 1.5 g of SA (GS) were solubilized in 100 mL of H2O for 2 h at 80 °C. The CQDs were added to the GS solution at concentrations of 1 %, 5 %, and 10 % (v/v), and the mixtures were stirred for 2 h to produce the GG + SA + CQDs (GSC) solutions. Subsequently, 2 mL of glycerol was applied to each mixed solution and stirred for 15 mins. The solutions (15 mL) were poured into the petri dishes (90 mm) and dried for 48 h at 30 °C. The films were labeled as GS, GSC1, GSC2, and GSC3, corresponding to the different CQDs concentrations.

The cross-sectional structure of GS and GSC films was captured with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL JSM-7800F, Japan). The film was freeze fractured using liquid nitrogen to create a clean cross section. Then it was affixed to an aluminum column and the sample was gold sprayed prior to imaging.

Photographs of the films under visible light and fluorescence were captured by using a Canon digital camera (EOS 1500D, Canon, Japan). For fluorescence imaging, samples were irradiated using 365 nm UV light.

The samples were placed inside the cuvette and tested for UV blocking properties using a spectrophotometer. At the same time, the samples were placed directly on the ATR accessory and the FTIR spectra were performed by using a Nicolet 50 spectrometer.

The thickness of the films was measured through digital micrometer at three random points with an accuracy of 0.1 μm. The elongation at break (EB) and tensile strength (TS) were evaluated by using a texture analyzer (TA96, Food Technology Co., VA, USA). Before the experiment, the film samples were cut into uniform strips. The initial length of the experiment was 30 mm and the stretching speed was 0.6 mm/s.
Water vapor permeability (WVP) was determined according to ASTM E96-05 standards, and the water contact angle (WCA) was measured with a WCA analyzer (KSV CAM20, Instruments Co., Ltd., Finland).

The thermogravimetric analysis（TGA） was performed using an SDT Q600 instrument (TA Instruments, USA) under a nitrogen atmosphere, with a temperature range of 30 ∼ 600 °C and a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The samples tested included GG, SA, GS, and GSC2 films. The activation energy was derived from the TGA curve based on Broido model (Alsulami & Rajeh, 2022) as shown in Eq. (2):

where wt is the weight at any time t; wi is the initial weight, w∞ is the final remaining weight, R is the gas constant (8.314 J·K-1mol−1), Ea is the activation energy, and T is temperature.

The Inhibition zone test is a proven method for assessing the antibacterial effectiveness of films against foodborne pathogens. The GSC samples were cut into 6 mm diameter discs and sterilized under UV light for 1 h. Bacterial suspensions of E. coli and S. aureus were prepared and diluted to an appropriate concentration (105 CFU/mL) using PBS buffer. 200 μL of each bacterial suspension was dispersed homogeneously onto sterilized nutrient agar plates. Sterilized film discs were then laid on the agar surface and incubated at 37 °C for 48 h (E. coli) or 24 h (S. aureus). The inhibition zones around each film disc were recorded after incubation. Simultaneously, the bacterial morphology was observed using SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Release kinetics of CQDs in the films
ce:section.ce:para:
The release kinetics of CQDs within the films were assessed to evaluate the release behavior during practical application. 10 mg sample of the GSC film was immersed in 30 mL of H2O and subjected to continuous shaking at 25 °C. 1 mL of the supernatant was withdrawn at predetermined time intervals and diluted to an appropriate concentration. The absorbance at 219 nm was then recorded. The CQDs concentration was calculated using a standard curve. The release kinetics were analyzed using several standard kinetic models, including zero-order (Zhang, Luo, Wang, Gu, & Lv, 2021), first-order (Bai, Cai, & Tian, 2019), Higuchi (Guan et al., 2024), and Ritger-Peppas models (Y. Li et al., 2025). The formula of the model is as follows:

where Mt is the cumulative amount of CQDs released at time t, M0 is the initial amount of CQDs, and k0 is the zero-order rate constant; k1 is the first-order rate constant; where kH is the Higuchi rate constant; where M∞ is the total amount of substance released, kn is the release rate constant, and n is the release exponent that indicates the mechanism of release.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications in the preservation of blanched asparagus
ce:section.ce:para: The edible portions of asparagus were cut into 6 cm sections. The cut pieces were blanched in hot water (100 °C) for 30 s and then cooled to 4 ± 1 °C. After blanching, the asparagus was immersed in water for 30 s and then air-dried on a rack. This group was the control group. For the treatment group, asparagus sections were fully immersed in the GSC2 solution for 30 s and then air-dried on a rack. The appearance changes of the asparagus were recorded and photographed.

To assess the quality of blanched asparagus during storage, weight loss, firmness, and browning index were measured. The initial (m0) and final (m1) weights of the asparagus were recorded, and weight loss was measured by the following formula:
（7）

To assess the water degradability of the GSC2 film, 0.01 g of film was taken from the sample surface and immersed in 20 mL of H2O. The films were placed in a room temperature (25 °C) and recorded the changes by photographs.

To validate the mechanism of CQDs in the preservation of blanched asparagus, the study examined the changes in chlorophyll content, peroxidase (POD) activity, and polyphenol oxidase (PPO) activity during storage. To measure the chlorophyll content, 2 g of the asparagus was mixed with 5 mL of 95 % EtOH solution and homogenized in an ice bath. The homogenate was then filtered and diluted to a final volume of 15 mL. The absorbance of the filtrate was measured at 665 nm and 649 nm (Nawaz et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2023). The total chlorophyll content was calculated according to the following equation:
（9）
Total chlorophyll content (mg/kg) =6.63
A 665
+18.08
A 649
To assess the POD and PPO activities, 1 g of the asparagus was mixed with 5 mL phosphate buffer (0.05 mol/L) and homogenized in an ice bath. The homogenate was centrifuged and the supernatant was collected. For POD activity, 0.5 mL of the supernatant was combined with 2.2 mL of a 1 % guaiacol solution and 0.2 mL of a 1.5 % hydrogen peroxide solution. A control was prepared with the same reagents without the supernatant. The absorbance at 470 nm was recorded every minute. For PPO activity, 0.5 mL of the supernatant was combined with 3 mL phosphate buffer and 1 mL of catechol solution (0.05 mol/L). A control was prepared by using the same mixture without the supernatant. The absorbance at 410 nm was recorded every minute. POD and PPO activities were calculated as enzyme activity units per gram of sample per minute for every 0.01 absorbance change (Ji, Wang, Lu, Ma, & Chen, 2022).
（10）

V 2
)
where V1 (mL) represents the volume of extract, t (min) represents response time, W (g) represents the sample weight, and V2 (mL) represents the extract used for measurement.

The results of all experiments are expressed as mean ± standard deviation and all experiments were performed in triplicate. Significant differences among data were evaluated using Duncan's multiple range test, with a significance set at P < 0.05. Figures and charts were generated and analyzed using Origin 2021 (OriginLab, Co., USA).

The size distribution and microstructure of CQDs were analyzed using TEM (Fig. 1 A). The CQDs exhibited a spherical morphology with uniform distribution and highly monodispersed, and the particle sizes were within the quantum range. The size distribution was fitted by using a Gaussian model with an average particle size of approximately 2.8 nm. The TEM imaging showed a clear lattice structure with a spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1B). Additionally, the zeta potential displayed a positive surface charge of approximately 7.89 mV. The small size and positive surface charge contribute to the high dispersibility of the CQDs in aqueous solution, enhancing their compatibility in film solutions (Priyadarshi, Riahi, Rhim, Han, & Lee, 2021).

The optical properties of lemon-based CQDs were investigated using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis spectrum of the CQDs exhibited a wide absorption range, which can be contributed to the random energy levels and complex band structure (Song et al., 2024). As illustrated in Fig. 1E, the observed absorption band at 295 nm belonged to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, while the absorption peak in the 350–400 nm range was associated with n-π* transitions of C-O bonds (Wang et al., 2022). The color changes of the CQDs solution under visible light and UV light (365 nm) were also illustrated in Fig. 1E. The CQDs is highly soluble in water, forming a well-dispersed solution. The CQDs solution emitted an intense blue fluorescence under UV light because of the quantum confinement. This rapid color change was the characteristic of CQDs from light emission in the UV spectrum (Shi et al., 2016).The luminescent performance of the CQDs was assessed by recording fluorescence emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths (350–420 nm). At 400 nm excitation wavelength, the CQDs displayed two prominent emission peaks at 475 nm and 520 nm (Fig. 1F). Additionally, the emission response revealed an excitation wavelength-dependent behavior, with a slight redshift of the emission peaks as the excitation wavelength increased. This fluorescence behavior was associated with surface passivation and the narrow size distribution of the CQDs (Jagannathan et al., 2021).

FTIR spectra confirmed the existence of functional groups on the CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1G, the peak at 3675 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups, while peaks at 1605, 1393, and 1066 cm−1 suggested the absence of carboxyl (–COO) groups. A broad band around 2988 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of saturated C–H bonds and the peak at 1250 cm−1 was due to C-N bonds (da Silva et al., 2023). These findings indicated the successful synthesis of lemon-based CQDs. The surface functional groups originated from organic compounds in lemons, such as vitamins (A, B6, B12, E) citric acid and ascorbic acid, which enriched the functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surface of the CQDs during the synthesis process (Ding, Zhou, Qin, Zhou, & Zhao, 2019). The enriched functional groups contributed to the antioxidant properties of CQDs by enabling electron donation for free radical reduction (da Silva et al., 2023). Additionally, the hydrophilic surface groups provide excellent water solubility, facilitating uniform dispersion in film solutions.

Free radical scavenging is a common method to assess the effectiveness of materials in neutralizing or scavenging free radicals. Antioxidants mitigate oxidative stress by neutralizing radicals, thereby preserving food quality and extending shelf life. Fig. 1H–I demonstrated the ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging efficiency of CQDs. Referring to previous studies, the study chose a concentration range of 1 to 512 ug/mL for the experiments (Hadavifar et al., 2025). At a concentration of 16 μg/mL, the free radical scavenging rates for ABTS was 12.2 %, while that for DPPH was 25.2 %. When the concentration was increased to 512 μg/mL, the scavenging efficiency significantly improved, with ABTS activity reaching 92.4 % and DPPH activity rising to 86.6 %. The color changed from blue (ABTS) and purple (DPPH) to yellow due to the formation of ABTS-H and DPPH-H complexes during the reaction. This shift occurred due to the rich polyphenols and flavonoids in lemon peel, which transfer functional groups (such as –OH, –NH2, –COOH) to the CQDs surface during carbonization process (Sul, Khan, & Rhim, 2024). These functional groups enable CQDs to scavenge free radicals by H-atoms transfer, which reduced the absorbance and changed the color (Khan, Riahi, Tae Kim, & Rhim, 2024). Moreover, CQDs showed more scavenging activity against ABTS compared to DPPH, which can be attributed to their superior hydrophilicity. This property augmented the solubility in the aqueous ABTS solution compared to the ethanol-based DPPH solution (Das et al., 2023). The results indicated that CQDs possess strong free radical scavenging ability, demonstrating their antioxidant potential.

The dispersion of CQDs within the GS-based composite film was evaluated through SEM images (Fig. 2 ). The cross-section of the GS film exhibited a uniform and smooth morphology, attributed to the strong hydrophilicity of GG and SA, which ensured good dispersion in water and excellent biocompatibility between the two polymer materials. The microstructure of the GSC films was similar to that of the GS film. FTIR analysis confirmed the presence of hydrophilic groups in the CQDs structure, which gave them a strong hydrophilicity. This characteristic facilitated the incorporation into the film matrix, preventing significant aggregation. Additionally, the functional groups on the CQDs surface may interact with the polymers, further promoting uniform dispersion in the GS matrix (Ezati et al., 2023). The extremely small particle size of CQDs (Fig. 1) also supported the even distribution in the film-forming material. SEM imaging showed that the cross-sectional structure of the GSC film was similar to that of GS film, indicating good CQDs dispersion within the biopolymer matrix. However, with the increase of CQDs concentration in the membrane, the cross section of the membrane became slightly rough. This observation suggested that although CQDs have good hydrophilicity, higher concentrations could adversely affect the structural integrity of the film.

The optical properties of films are crucial in determining the visual appeal of food products and influencing consumer acceptance. The appearance of the GS and GSC films were shown in Fig. 2. The SEM image of the surface of the composite films appeared smooth. The GS film was colorless and transparent under daylight. However, the color of the films changed from transparent to yellow with the addition of CQDs. The color deepened to a brownish-yellow as the CQDs concentration increased. Under UV excitation at 365 nm, the GS film showed no fluorescence, while the GSC films exhibited increasing fluorescence intensity as CQDs concentrations rose. This indicated the successful incorporation of CQDs into the film without compromising their fundamental properties. The emission intensity of the entire film surface is consistent, which further confirms the uniform distribution of CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 3 A, the FTIR spectral revealed the chemical interactions in the GS-based films after adding CQDs. In the GS film, a characteristic peak around 3343 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of the –OH group. The absorption peak near 2924 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of alkyl C–H bonds. An absorption peak at 1601 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of CO bonds, and absorption peaks at 1080 and 1029 cm−1 related to the stretching vibrations of C–O and C–C–C bonds, respectively (Riahi, Khan, Rhim, Shin, & Kim, 2024). FTIR analysis revealed shifts in the –OH stretching vibration peaks of the biopolymer matrix after CQDs incorporation, with characteristic peaks shifting from 3343 cm−1 in the GS film to 3304–3362 cm−1 in the GSC films. These shifts indicate the formation of hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl groups of GG and the carboxyl groups of SA, as well as with the functional groups on the CQDs. This molecular integration also likely explains the enhanced mechanical strength observed in the GSC films, as the CQDs serve to reinforce the polymer network, creating a more stable structure (Hu et al., 2021). In addition, FTIR spectra of different regions of GSC films were tested. The FTIR spectra showed that the peaks and intensities of the functional groups remained consistent in different regions of the film, which indicated that the doping of CQDs was uniform.

The chlorophyll and the enzymes are highly sensitive to UV radiation, making the UV-blocking performance of coating films is crucial (Jayakumar, Radoor, Shin, & Kim, 2024). The UV-blocking properties of the GSC films were measured by their ability to absorb UV light below 400 nm, with a significant increase in UV-blocking capacity observed in the films containing CQDs compared to unmodified GS films (Fig. 3B). As the CQDs concentration increased, the transmittance of the GSC films decreased markedly, indicating the enhanced light-blocking properties. Specifically, the transmittance at 280 nm (T280) decreased from 38.94 to 1.36 %, and (T660) decreased from 85.48 to 68.75 % at 660 nm. The greater reduction at T280 suggested that the excellent UV-blocking properties of CQDs. The CQDs, with their high absorbance in the UV spectrum, effectively reduce the transmission of harmful UV radiation, protecting food from UV-induced degradation, such as vitamin loss and lipid oxidation (Jayakumar et al., 2024). This UV-blocking ability is consistent with previous studies that have demonstrated the efficacy of CQDs as UV-absorbing agents in food packaging systems (Riahi, Rhim, Bagheri, Pircheraghi, & Lotfali, 2022). The enhanced UV-blocking properties of the CQD-loaded films provide a dual function of extending the shelf life of MPFVs by not only preventing browning and microbial growth but also safeguarding food quality against light-induced damage.

Packaging films with excellent mechanical properties provide better protection against external physical damage and ensure food integrity. Therefore, the physical properties of the GSC were evaluated (Fig. 3C). The thickness of the GSC film decreased with the increasing concentration of CQDs. The GSC3 film exhibited the smallest thickness at 0.081 mm. This phenomenon may be attributed to the influence of CQDs on solvent evaporation and the drying process during film formation. Higher CQDs concentration might influence the solvent evaporation rate and the shrinkage behavior during the drying, leading to variations in thickness (Khan et al., 2024).TS and EB are critical parameters for evaluating the mechanical properties of films. As illustrated in Fig. 3D, the GS film showed high flexibility, with a TS of 27.20 MPa and an EB of 5.61 %. The incorporation of CQDs enhanced the tensile strength of the films, with GSC2 demonstrating the highest TS of 38.80 MPa. This indicates that although the SEM images of GSC films show an uneven cross section, the hydrogen bond interaction between CQDs and the substrate outweighed any negative effects caused by the uneven morphology. At the highest CQD concentration (GSC3), a slight decrease in TS was observed, likely due to the uneven cross-sectional morphology and potential aggregation of CQDs at high concentrations. Despite this, the GS matrix's flexibility and ductility maintained acceptable elongation at break (EB) values. However, due to the inherently flexibility and ductility of the GS matrix, the films retained good extensibility with the increase of TS. Consequently, the addition of CQDs did not significantly impact the EB value (Khan et al., 2024).

An appropriate WVP could regulate the humidity in the film, preventing food from becoming overly dry or damp, thereby preserving its texture, taste, and extending shelf life. The GS film exhibited a WVP of 4.953 × 10−7 g·m−1·h−1·Pa−1. The WVP decreased slightly with the increasing concentration of CQDs (Fig. 1E). This phenomenon was consistent with previous studies, indicating that the addition of hydrophilic CQDs did not significantly affect the WVP of hydrophilic biopolymer films (Pereira & Andrade, 2017). A slight decrease in WVP means that the water vapor permeability of the packaging film is diminished, which reduces the exchange of moisture between the inside and outside of the package. Water retention is especially important for MPFVs. For high-moisture foods, the package can better retain internal moisture and slow the rate of drying and water loss, thereby extending the moisture retention and freshness of the food. At the same time, a decrease in WVP reduces moisture activity inside the package, which can inhibit microbial colonization to some extent.

The surface hydrophilicity of the films was assessed by using WCA measurements. The WCA less than 90° indicates a hydrophilic surface, while the values above 90° suggest a hydrophobic surface. The greater hydrophobicity corresponding to the larger angles (Huang et al., 2019). The GS film exhibited a WCA of 45.36° (Fig. 1F), confirming its strong hydrophilicity. The surface hydrophilicity of the GSC films did not show significant changes, with the WCA of 48.35° (GSC1), 45.25° (GSC2), and 45.84° (GSC3). This phenomenon attributed to the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs. This groups contribute to the overall hydrophilicity of the films. Previous studies have also demonstrated that good surface hydrophilicity could enhance the adherence of the solution on food surface and improve the adhesion properties (Khoshkalampour, Ghorbani, & Ghasempour, 2023).

Table 1 summarizes the TGA and DTG degradation results of the four films. The thermal stability of different films can be effectively compared by the peak DTG temperature and the activation energy calculated by the Briodo model. Specifically, the change in DTG peak temperature during the volatilization stage indicates that the peak temperatures of the GS and GSC films increased from 169.36 °C to 204.61 °C (GS) and 196.45 ℃ (GSC), respectively, suggesting an improvement in their thermal stability. Moreover, the activation energies of the GS and GSC films are significantly higher than those of the GG and SA films, indicating that the composite films require more energy for thermal decomposition, thereby demonstrating superior thermal stability. Overall, the GS and GSC films exhibit significantly better thermal stability than the GG and SA films, highlighting their greater potential for application (Lotfy, Basta, Abdel-Monem, & Abdel-Hamed, 2021). Additionally, the higher activation energy may contribute to slowing the release of active components from the films, effectively preventing rapid release and enhancing the long-lasting and durable properties of the films (Basta, Lotfy, & Eldewany, 2021).

The ABTS and DPPH assays were employed to evaluate the radical scavenging activity of CQDs due to their high sensitivity and reproducibility under controlled conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A–B, the GS film exhibited antioxidant activities of 9.35 % and 17.25 % against DPPH and ABTS radicals, respectively. The radical scavenging ability may be attributed to the formation of stable macromolecular radicals and ammonium groups between the residual free amino groups in SA reacting with DPPH or ABTS radicals. The radical scavenging activities of the GSC film improved significantly, indicating superior antioxidant performance compared to the GS film. This enhancement was due to the synergistic antioxidant effect between CQDs and GS. CQDs possess excellent radical scavenging abilities due to their abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups, which can donate electrons to neutralize free radicals (Zou et al., 2024). To further evaluate the stability of antioxidant of the film during extended storage, we stored the GSC film at 4 °C for 14 days and measured its antioxidant performance at 7-day intervals. As shown in Fig. S2, although the antioxidant performance of the GSC film decreased slightly over the 14-day storage period, the reduction was not significant. This result indicated that CQDs exhibit good long-term stability under low-temperature conditions, effectively maintaining their antioxidant activity over an extended storage period, thereby ensuring the functionality of the active packaging. When compared to other active packaging films reported in the literature (Table S1), the Lemon-CQDs-based GSC film exhibited competitive antioxidant properties. This superior performance makes the GSC film a promising candidate for active packaging applications, effectively mitigating oxidative stress and enhancing the shelf life of food products.

Bacteria produce organic acids, aldehydes, ketones, and other compounds during metabolism, which can react with food components, leading to color changes and nutrient loss (Moradi et al., 2021). Therefore, controlling microbial growth during food storage is an essential demand. S. aureus (a Gram-positive bacterium) and E. coli (a Gram-negative bacterium) were selected as test strains due to their prevalence as foodborne pathogens. Fig. 4C displayed the inhibition zones of the sample types against S. aureus and E. coli. According to previous studies, antimicrobial effectiveness is classified based on inhibition zone diameters (Moradi et al., 2021). The GSC3 film demonstrated inhibition zones of 15.45 mm for S. aureus and 18.94 mm for E. coli, indicating the higher sensitive antimicrobial effects. This effectiveness was due to the ability of CQDs to disrupt bacterial biofilms and cell membranes through reactions with OH− and O2 − (Kuo et al., 2024). Notably, the inhibition zone for S. aureus was larger than that for E. coli. This difference was attributed to the more complex cell wall structure of Gram- negative bacteria. The lipopolysaccharide-rich outer membrane of E. coli hindered CQDs penetration, whereas Gram-positive bacteria like S. aureus lacked this barrier, allowing easier CQDs access to the cell membrane (Zhao et al., 2023). However, the film still effectively disrupted the membranes of Gram-negative bacteria (as shown in Fig. 4D), demonstrating the broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity. Additionally, the positive charged of CQDs enhances adhesion to the negatively charged bacterial surfaces, further enhancing its antimicrobial activity. A comparative analysis (Table S2) revealed that the inhibition zone diameters of GSC3 surpassed other reported active packaging films. This suggests that the GSC3 film offers enhanced antimicrobial efficacy, making it a promising candidate for food preservation applications.

The release of CQDs was quantified using food simulants (Fig. 5 A–F). Water was chosen as the simulant for vegetable-based foods. Upon immersion in the simulant, a minor molecular diffusion process occurred. CQDs was released from the polymer matrix due to this diffusion mechanism. In the aqueous environment, the pronounced concentration gradient between the solution and film caused an initial burst release within the first 30 mins. A stabilization period followed, after which the release rate remained constant. All films exhibited a similar release trend. The final release rate of the GSC3 film was relatively low due to the hydrogen bonding between the polymer and CQDs, which resisted further release (Alzahrani, 2024).

The release kinetics of CQDs were simulated using zero-order, first-order, and Higuchi models. The correlation coefficients (R2) indicated that CQDs release followed first-order kinetics, confirming its diffusion-driven behavior. Additionally, the Ritger-Pappas model was employed to further analyze the release behavior of CQDs. The release exponent (n) less than 0.50 for all films, which indicated the Fickian diffusion rule. This suggested that the release mechanism is primarily driven by concentration gradients rather than dissolution or diffusion processes (Guan et al., 2024).

Based on the experimental results, the GSC2 film was selected for a preservation experiment involving blanched asparagus. The coated asparagus was stored at 4 °C. Throughout the storage period, the asparagus samples were monitored for appearance, weight loss, firmness, and browning index. As shown in Fig. 6 A, all samples exhibited good firmness and color on the beginning. However, the green color gradually faded in all groups as storage progressed. Fig. 6B–D revealed that the control group displayed more severe shrinkage and deeper color changes compared to the treated group. While both groups experienced increasing weight loss over time. The rate of weight loss on day 8 was significantly higher in the control group than in the treatment group. Similarly, the firmness of the samples decreased over time, with a sharper reduction in the control group. However, the treated group maintained greater firmness, particularly during the later stages (days 6–8). Additionally, the browning index increased throughout the storage period, indicating a change in the color of the samples. The treated group consistently showed a lower browning index than the control group, with a more pronounced difference on days 6–8, highlighting the superior ability of film to inhibit browning.

The removability of a coating from food surfaces through washing is a key indicator of its safety and environmental impact. As depicted in Fig. S1, the GSC2 film began to disintegrate gradually after being immersed in water for approximately 60 s. This phenomenon showed that the film could be simply removed from the asparagus surface by washing. After 1 h, the film fragmented into smaller pieces, and it had further degraded by 1.5 h. With gentle agitation, the film completely dissolved in water. These results demonstrated the excellent water degradability and solubility of the GSC2 film. This suggested that the film can be easily washed off food surfaces, underscoring its biocompatibility and environmental safety.

The color changes in asparagus during storage are primarily influenced by chlorophyll degradation, browning reactions, and the activity of endogenous enzymes. In order to investigate the coating inhibits mechanism, the total chlorophyll content, PPO, and POD activity were measured throughout storage. As depicted in Fig. 6E, the total chlorophyll content gradually decreased over time in all samples. However, the treatment group consistently maintained higher chlorophyll content compared to the control group, indicating the effectiveness of film in preserving the green color. This observation was consistent with the browning index. The treatment group maintained the visual quality of the samples more effectively by delaying browning through the inhibition of chlorophyll degradation.

PPO activity increased over time but stabilized after day 4. Throughout the storage period, the treatment group exhibited slightly lower PPO activity than the control group, especially during the later stages. The results suggested that the coating was more effective inhibition on PPO activity, thereby slowing browning and reducing quality deterioration (Fig. 6F–G). POD activity fluctuated slightly during storage without a significant trend. Nevertheless, the POD activity in the treatment group was lower than that in the control group at most time points, which may be related to the ability of the treatment to delay quality deterioration. In addition, the antioxidant properties of CQDs themselves can effectively neutralize free radicals and inhibit the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), thus reducing the occurrence of browning (Qin et al., 2024). Meanwhile, the UV-blocking property of the film further protects food quality by preventing UV-induced degradation of chlorophyll and other sensitive compounds in asparagus (Wang et al., 2022). In summary, the retardation of browning of blanched asparagus was the result of multiple mechanisms.

This study successfully developed a lemon-based CQDs-enhanced guar gum/sodium alginate (GSC) film with significant potential for improving the shelf life of MPFVs. The incorporation of CQDs significantly improved the UV-blocking capability, antioxidant activity, and antimicrobial properties of the films. The release kinetics of CQDs from films followed Fickian diffusion. FTIR and SEM analyses confirmed strong interactions between CQDs and the GS matrix. In a preservation experiment with blanched asparagus, the GSC2 film effectively mitigated quality deterioration by minimizing weight loss, retaining firmness, and lowering the browning index. Additionally, the film preserved chlorophyll content and inhibited PPO and POD activity, effectively delaying browning and maintaining the visual appeal. Overall, the use of natural, renewable materials like lemon peel-derived CQDs offers a sustainable alternative to conventional active packaging systems. However, the research focused primarily on a single food product (blanched asparagus), and the long-term stability and performance of the films on a wider variety of food products were not fully explored. Future research should include a broader range of food types to assess the general applicability of the CQD-loaded films. Additionally, further investigations into the long-term stability of CQDs, their potential migration into food, and the scalability of the production process are essential for advancing this technology toward commercial use.
